text: Three small incisions are made around the umbilicus, and ports are inserted into these incisions. Purse-string sutures are placed around the ports, and the robotic system is brought into the operative field and secured to the ports.
Three ports are inserted through a single incision using a SILS port, with the retracting instrument positioned superior to the dissecting instrument to minimize arm collision during the procedure.

The surgeon isolates the ileocolic pedicle as the first step in the single incision right colon procedure. The omentum is moved to the upper abdomen, and a hook cautery is used to dissect the ileocolic vessels. The duodenum is identified traveling underneath the dissected area.

The surgeon introduces a ligature through one of the robotic ports or a laparoscopic trocar. The robotic arm remains docked to the port, with the instrument removed, allowing the use of a handheld ligature. After transecting the pedicle, the focus shifts to lateral mobilization of the colon.
In the robotic version of the procedure, the surgeon uses a hook electrocautery device, while in the standard laparoscopy, scissors with cautery or a ligature are employed for tissue dissection.

In the laparoscopic procedure, the surgeon uses a ligature to mobilize the hepatic flexure. In the robotic procedure, the duodenum is being mobilized off the colonic mesentery, with all lateral attachments already taken down.
The surgeon extracts the specimens from the abdominal cavity and performs an extracorporeal anastomosis, connecting the bowel segments outside the body.
